sentences.text: Well, first let me say that, after my first home with the young Frenchwoman and young British woman, then I wasn't there very long before I moved to a large mansion on Hebelstrasse-- three full levels-- where I lived with two American court reporters, Ann Daniels and Dorothy Fitzgerald, who were good friends.
We became very good friends.
And this house was right across the street from the house in-- on Hebelstrasse-- in which-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] It's back here.
This was the house on Hebelstrasse, in which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Spencer Tracy was filmed, Judge-- filmed-- was filmed in a huge home.
And then after that, we tried to get into the Grand Hotel as soon as we could.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went-- I lived in the Grand Hotel on the fourth floor, where the greatest security was.
From there, it was easy for us to, to get together in the lobby of the Grand Hotel.
Now remember, I told you that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was no heat anywhere.
So we didn't have any hot water to take baths or showers in, only ice cold water, and no heat at all.
We had feather beds.

And in the Grand Hotel, even before I moved there, when we got out of court, what we had heard during that day was so horrifying that none of us wanted to go back to our particular rooms in my first home to be alone at night.
And therefore, we all-- and we had to go somewhere for an army mess.
And in, in the big mansion, the army actually had a mess in our grand ballroom on the fourth-- first floor.
I don't know who owned-- which-- what Germans were displaced, you know, who owned that house.
But in the Grand Hotel, of course, we had the, the dining room there.
And we would all meet in the lobby at the Grand Hotel after 5 o'clock.
And we were taken there by a US Army bus always, because of the security.
We, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we probably had [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a-- we probably had the opportunity to go various places.
I mean, I know that we did have.
But we had to have orders cut.

If it was a long weekend where the counsel were preparing for the next segment of testimony-- Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, for example-- we could go to Prague, or wherever in our little group we decided we'd like to go that weekend.
It could be-- it could've been in Germany, and was many times-- to resort areas in Germany-- Garmisch, Berchtesgaden, which was where Hitler's Eagle's Nest was.
And our favorite place was Prague, Czechoslovakia.
And we would go on a train with our orders, German train, maybe about 20 of us.
And we would spend the weekend in Prague.
And I remember on one occasion, the last-- we loved Prague.
We loved the people.
Because if we didn't know where we were going, where we wanted to go in the old city, they would walk 10 blocks with us to show us where we wanted to go.
And we would-- they, they never had dinner served in the hotels in Prague, or anywhere in Europe, until 8:00 PM.
That was the dinner hour-- 9:00 PM, 10:00 PM.
They had these wonderful Viennese orchestras, you know, that played during the dinner hour.
And we wouldn't get back to our rooms in the hotel until 1:00 or 2:00 in the morning.
And I can remember the last trip I made to Prague before the coup.
I didn't even turn my, my Czech money, koruna, in, which we were supposed to do.
Because I was going to go back the next weekend with our group, my friends, a few court reporters and a few press people.
But I could not go that next weekend.
And the others went.
And that was the weekend that the coup occurred.
And they told me that there were machine guns on every corner of the hotel.
And they were all arrested.
And they were kept there for 48 hours under interrogation.
Now, that is an experience that I'm sorry I missed.
Because I knew they all came back very well.
But after that point in time, there were many Czech employees on the Allied payroll.
The communists had taken over.
And they had these awful decisions to make about, are they going to go back home to Czechoslovakia, to Prague, where their parents were, their families, under communism?
Or were they going to not go back?
So many of them made decisions not to go.
Now, was there any time where you felt threatened while you were working on the Nuremburg trials?
In the Grand Hotel-- across from the Grand Hotel was the walled city of Nuremberg, which had been bombed, almost flattened.
All the time that I was in Nuremberg, there were bathtubs hanging from the walls of the bombed out city.
Nuremberg was bombed.
But the walled city was really destroyed.
And there were still unrecovered bodies all the time I was there.
The stench of death was everywhere.
And I lived in the Grand Hotel for most of the time I was there, on the fourth floor.

And one afternoon, as I and another court reporter, Edna Malloy from Omaha, were preparing to go down to dinner, about 5 o'clock in the Grand Hotel, we heard and felt an enormous vibration, an explosion.
And I knew the hotel had been bombed.
We had had bomb drills before.
Hotels had been bombed in Stuttgart by the Nazi fanatics, who are still hiding, where Allied and US people lived.
And so we rushed down three flights of stairs, meeting everyone else in the hotel rushing out.

The Nazi fanatics had come out from under the walled city, the catacombs-- I don't know how many-- and thrown a bomb-- crossed the street and thrown a bomb into the big, glass-- pardon me-- windows of the Grand Hotel into the dining room.
And it hit against a wall, over which two people had just departed.
But the dining room was crowded.
And no one was killed.
But there were injuries from flying glass and debris.
So after that, the security around the Grand Hotel, which was a, you know, top security pass to get in beyond our American MPs at the front desk, one entrance, was tightly secured, too.
And of course, we were never allowed to go out for this very reason.
Because they would come out of the catacombs and shoot anybody on the street who looked like someone on the Allied payroll, yes.
This was the hazard that we knew we had to live with.
And it was part of our original orientation, if you will, to know that we were restricted.
You mentioned previously that you married an American MP.
Yes.
Yes.

It was a very romantic time to be there, and because of the environment, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the fact that we were a small, American community, and the American MPs there, and American lawyers, and American, you know, whoever they were.
But I married a, a military police officer who was in charge of the three major Nazi leaders who were acquitted in Goering's trial, von Papen, Fritzsche, and Schacht, I think, were the other two.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so that was a happy circumstance for us
Ellis Elmer Spitz.
Yes.
Yes.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Well it was-- I guess we met, all of us together.
We were-- there was nothing very private about any, [LAUGHS] any of this.
Because we were all-- and there were others, you know.
My friend, [?
Alani, ?] Piulani Ahuna married Ziggy Ramler, the interpreter I mentioned in another segment.
And she was a court reporter on our team.
We were such good friends.
And I guess we all kind of found a partner, partner at that time.
But we met every night in-- after work in the Grand Hotel lobby, where we sat.
And we had a couple of drinks, tried to find hors d'oevres.
It was a Grand Hotel-- hors d'oevres.
And occasionally, we had one man who was American in charge of the PX, who had an electric heater.
There was heat in fireplaces and nowhere else.
And he plugged that heater into a wall in the courtroom.
And he was the most popular man in the lobby.
So-- and he was also a friend of ours.
And from there, we went into the dining room for dinner, Grand Hotel.
I, I know that we did.
I, I know that we didn't want to be alone.
And that's why we all congregated together.
And I know that we, we talked about something that may have happened that day during the trial.
We also talked an awful lot about our lives in the countries where we came from, which was interesting.
I mean, we bonded with some of the British.
And we talked about our lives elsewhere, before we ever got there, and things that happened, you know.
What we were going to do when we returned, plans for the future, so--
Yes, I was involved in-- I went in and out of, I think, certainly three of the trials subsequently.
But as opposed to the doctors' trial, which I reported from the beginning to the end with these other reporters, I went in and out on a daily assignment basis into these other three courtrooms.
And I do not have the-- you know, a great deal of memory, or I have not retained the knowledge, too much, of what went on in those, those-- in that testimony.
But one was the IG Farben Industry trial, which was the United States of America versus Karl Krauch-- K-A-R-L K-R-A-U-C-H.

And another was the United States of America versus Alfried Krupp von Bohlen, von Bohlen, und Halbach, who Alfried Krupp was of the munitions family, and the son of the old Gustav, Krupp, who was initially indicted in the major Nazi trial by mistake.
He was an 85-year-old man who had stroke.
And so rather than substitute the son for him in that major trial, they tried him subsequently.
And I know I was in another trial that involved the Gestapo.
But I do not know which of those titles covered that trial.
Yes.
It was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] May of 1948.
And it was a chaotic period of time there.
I had a one-year contract, which had to have been up sometime around the end of October, '47.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they wa-- they did not want us to leave.
Because that meant they had to recruit other reporters to go over.
All 13 trials took from November 1945 through beyond-- shortly beyond April of 1949, when the last judgments were rendered in April.
And they did not want us to leave.
But at that time, the Berlin Airlift was going on.
And there was a certain amount of anxiety over, are the Russians coming, to the extent that all travel and transportation provided by the US War Department for any of us was canceled.
And they were-- they got out all of the Allied personnel wives and children from Berlin because of the Berlin crisis over the Russians.
The Russians were no longer part of the trial process.
But they were still part of the four-power [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sectioned off areas of Germany.
And of course, they had Berlin.
They had a great, great part of Ber-- Berlin.
Well, I actually made the decision that-- I-- some of us actually told our, our superiors, no, we are not going to start another trial.
Because we think if we do, then you are not going to try to get us home.
And that-- but that was not a heavy kind of handed-- heavy-handed thing on our part.
We wanted to get home.

And I remember, actually, my husband and I going to Brussels and Paris, in particular, to see if I could buy my way on a plane or a ship out of France or Brussels-- a plane, Bremerhaven, Germany, pay my own way.
There was no way for any private citizen, German or any nationality, to get out of Europe for, for the US.
Because every passage on every ship and plane had already been booked.
So eventually-- I went on waiting lists.
And eventually, I was able to get a cancellation-- and I'm sure it was out of Bremerhaven-- by ship.
Because I know I came home on the SS United States.
Well, it had to be in late May, 1948.
Before that, Gary, this particular ship [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had a great many Jewish refugees in the hold, or the bottom deck.
They were ref-- political refugees.
They were refugees from, especially, for example, Czechoslovakia, going to the United States.
And I can remember that they did not have their kosher food.
And they weren't eating.
And that came to the attention of the ship's captain so that he could remedy that by providing-- and I'm not that familiar with the diet, you know, for the, the Orthodox Jew.
But he managed to work with the Jewish people on that ship so that he could provide food for them.
It was a long trip-- 12 days, I think.
It may have been more than 12 days, but at least that I know.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when, when we came into New York Harbor, [CRYING] and all of the Jews were on deck, [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] at the sight of the Statue of Liberty, they all fell on their knees, sobbing.
[CRYING] And I was right with them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That scene, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] too, was one of the most important scenes that maybe has affected my life after that, and made me so Jewish aware.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then we got to New York.
And we had a German girl on board that ship, who learned that I had reported the Nuremberg war trials.
And she was adamant that it was all propaganda by the United States and Allied countries-- all propaganda, every bit of it.
And she was not-- she was a young woman who might have been in her 30s, you know, at that time.
So that was my first encounter with Holocaust denial.
And I couldn't believe it.
And then I got to New York, and you know, from that point on.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my older son-- our older son, was born in New York.
And I was there, waiting for my husband.
And he arrived a couple months-- I was there with friends.
So I, I did have a period of time in New York.
And then, when he arrived, we went back to Detroit, to my mother's home.
He was in the military.
He would be awaiting his next military assignment.
And he managed to have it be in New York-- I mean, in Detroit.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
At that time, we were not in Detroit very long when he, he went-- we went to Lansing, Michigan shortly after our arrival, where he entered Michigan State College for some kind of a military course.
And while we were in Lansing, I worked for the Bar Association, the State Bar Association, not as a court reporter, but in their office.
And we were there for not a long period of time-- '48?
I have to check my notes.
Well, I traveled-- we traveled every year after that, going from one military base to another for his assignment.
And I became, pretty much, a, a career military officer wife, with all of the attendant duties, which was not a happy thing for me.
I mean, that was not my, you know, the-- my plans for life to hold, have coffees for officers' wives at military bases.
And I soon learned how to get out of that sort of thing.
Then he was assigned to Hart, Michigan, for a new artillery base opening there.
And that was 1949, I think.
And we lived on the lake.
It was a marvelous duty, if you will, for military, and then back on his military duty in Detroit for a period of time.
And I did not work in the court system again for several years. 1951, I think it was, well, he was assigned to Fort Leonard Wood in Missouri.
And it was there that the Judge Advocate General learned that there was a live court reporter had moved into the Fort Leonard Wood area and contacted me.
And I worked courts-martial with a very nice arrangement with them, where I could do all the transcript at home-- the murder trials in the courts-martial, Fort Leonard Wood.
And about 1952, we went to Fort Riley, Kansas on his military duty, from where he went to Korea and was a front line officer over there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back to Detroit, to my mother's dur-- for this period of time while he was in Korea.
And at that time, our younger son was born, Peter Alan, who is now in petroleum engineer down in Houston.
My older son is here in Denver, John Kimberly.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when he came back from Korea, then we, then we-- he, he, he was assigned to be a military instructor at Culver Military Academy in Culver, Indiana-- very exclusive Military Academy.
And we went there.
And then from-- I did not work the court system anywhere there.
And from there, because our younger son was a severe asthmatic, he acquired duty, upon the doctor's recommendations, for Denver, Colorado.
